title: Colonialism and Response
description: This exercise will cover the formation of new states as a response to imperialism and the rise of the global economy through the themes GOV, ENV, and ECN. Each theme will have a model and each model will scale 3 items, for a total of 12.
topic_path: Indigenous Responses to State Expansion from 1750 to 1900 CE | 6.3

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best illustrates a way in which indigenous people resisted colonialism from 1750 to 1900 CE?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: moving around
petitioning other countries
creating competing joint-stock companies
nationalizing resources

assessment_items.question_content:
**Resistance in European colonies came in many different forms. Which of the following examples of colonial resistance most relates to political authority?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: In French Indochina, local leaders pretended to comply with colonial officials while secretly conducting business as usual.
In the Dutch East Indies, people moved around in order to avoid taxes and forced labor.
In Spanish Latin America, workers in copper mines organized labor unions that advocated for safer working conditions.
In British West Africa, Asante officials lied about the availability of natural resources in order to broker more favorable trade deals.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>How can a proud and brave people like the Asante sit back and look while white men took away their king and chiefs, and humiliated them with a demand for the Golden Stool. The Golden Stool only means money to the whitemen; they have searched and dug everywhere for it. I shall not pay one predwan to the governor. If you, the chiefs of Asante, are going to behave like cowards and not fight, you should exchange your loincloths for my undergarments.

Source: Yaa Asantewaa, Speech to Asante Councilmembers in response to British colonization in West Africa, 1895.  

**Which of the following forms of colonial resistance is being described in the excerpt above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: armed rebellion
moving around
stealing goods
use of satire

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>You made me a promise, but henceforth [from here onwards] I shall not heed your promises, for you did not keep your word. ...I gave you plenty of warnings to march immediately on Cuzco, but you took them all lightly, giving the Spaniards time to prepare as they have done, placing cannon on Picchu Mountain, and devising other measures so dangerous that you are no longer in a position to attack them. ... God must want me to suffer my sins. Your wife. 

>[P.S] After I had finished this letter, a messenger arrived with the definite news that the enemy from Paruro is in Acos; I am going forward to attack them, even if it costs me my life

Source: Letter from Micaela Bastidas to her husband Tupac Amaru II, the leader of a rebellion against the Spanish in modern-day Peru. 1780. 

**Which of the following actions are most similar to those described in the excerpt above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the 1857 Rebellion in India
the establishment of the Cherokee Nation
the Ghost Dance movement
the movement of people in Java

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>Not content with imparting to his people a knowledge of the sciences, and the arts of war, Mohammad Ali, in 1819, was led by the advice of Europeans to attempt the introduction of the manufacturing system. In this undertaking he was deluded by fallacious calculations. Being persuaded that, with the aid of certain French and Swiss adventurers, it was possible to render Cairo a second Manchester, he commenced operations with his usual rapidity.

>... The cultivation of the cotton-plant already promised to become a fertile source of revenue. He now, therefore, determined to manufacture from the raw material, and [converted the factory into] a cotton-mill ... [and] caused cotton-mills to be erected at ... Mansoura, and in the southern parts of the metropolis. ... 

>... Of the twenty-three or twenty-four cotton-mills existing in Egypt, there is not one which has not, at various periods, been accidentally or designedly set on fire. ...At present most of the mils are in ruins, and immense heaps of machinery, no longer employed, are covered with rust, and moldering to decay. …

Source: James Augustus St. John (1795–1875), a British journalist, writer and traveler, *Egypt and Mohammed Ali: Or, Travels in the Valley of the Nile*, 1834.

**Which of the following historical developments is depicted in the excerpt above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the establishment of export-based economies in colonies
the development of a rail system in Africa
the success of cotton manufacturing in Egypt
the beginning of armed rebellions against colonial authority

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>Not content with imparting to his people a knowledge of the sciences, and the arts of war, Mohammad Ali, in 1819, was led by the advice of Europeans to attempt the introduction of the manufacturing system. In this undertaking he was deluded by fallacious calculations. Being persuaded that, with the aid of certain French and Swiss adventurers, it was possible to render Cairo a second Manchester, he commenced operations with his usual rapidity.

>... The cultivation of the cotton-plant already promised to become a fertile source of revenue. He now, therefore, determined to manufacture from the raw material, and [converted the factory into] a cotton-mill ... [and] caused cotton-mills to be erected at ... Mansoura, and in the southern parts of the metropolis. ... 

>... Of the twenty-three or twenty-four cotton-mills existing in Egypt, there is not one which has not, at various periods, been accidentally or designedly set on fire. ...At present most of the mils are in ruins, and immense heaps of machinery, no longer employed, are covered with rust, and moldering to decay. …

Source: James Augustus St. John (1795–1875), a British journalist, writer and traveler, *Egypt and Mohammed Ali: Or, Travels in the Valley of the Nile*, 1834. 

**Which of the following is a cause of the historical development depicted in the excerpt above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the  European need for raw materials
the establishment of the first colonies in Africa
the manufacturing boom in Egypt
the formation of trade routes in Africa

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>We slept at the gold mines of Yaquil near Rancagua, in the possession of Mr. Nixon, an American gentleman…When we arrived at the mine, I was struck by the pale appearance of many of the men…The mine is altogether 450 feet deep, each man brings up on his back ...104 lbs weight of stone…Even beardless young men of 18 & 20 years with little muscular development of their bodies carry this great load from nearly the same depth... 

>With this very severe labor they are allowed only beans & bread...Their pay is 25 or 30 shillings per month. — They only leave the mine once in three weeks, when they remain with their families two days. — This treatment, bad as it sounds, is gladly accepted; the state of the laboring agriculturist is much worse…

Source: Charles Darwin’s Observation of Copper Mining in Chile from the Beagle Diary, 1835. 

**The interactions described in the above excerpt are best understood in the broad context of which of the following historical themes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the violent nature of colonialism
the rise of the copper industry
the strategic movement of people in Latin America
the Columbian Exchange

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>We slept at the gold mines of Yaquil near Rancagua, in the possession of Mr. Nixon, an American gentleman…When we arrived at the mine, I was struck by the pale appearance of many of the men…The mine is altogether 450 feet deep, each man brings up on his back ...104 lbs weight of stone…Even beardless young men of 18 & 20 years with little muscular development of their bodies carry this great load from nearly the same depth... 

>With this very severe labor they are allowed only beans & bread...Their pay is 25 or 30 shillings per month. — They only leave the mine once in three weeks, when they remain with their families two days. — This treatment, bad as it sounds, is gladly accepted; the state of the laboring agriculturist is much worse…

Source: Charles Darwin’s Observation of Copper Mining in Chile from the Beagle Diary, 1835. 

**A historian would most likely use the above excerpt to illustrate which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how colonial authorities used brutality to drive profits
how colonies were first established in the Americas
the methods indigenous peoples used to resist colonial authority
the benefits many indigenous people experienced under colonialism

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best identifies countries' primary motivations for practicing economic imperialism from 1750 to 1900 CE?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: protecting their interests, increasing their influence, and because a military invasion isn’t feasible
civilizing undeveloped communities, sharing wealth, and gaining knowledge
building military bases, converting others to Christianity, and fighting communism
the White Man’s Burden,  manufacturing cheaply, securing naval superiority

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>All those people in China who sell opium or smoke opium should receive the death penalty. If we trace the crime of those barbarians who through the years have been selling opium, then the deep harm they have wrought and the great profit they have usurped should fundamentally justify their execution according to law…

>We find that your country is sixty or seventy thousand li [three li equal one mile] from China. Yet there are barbarian ships that strive to come here for trade for the purpose of making a great profit. The wealth of China is used to profit the barbarians. That is to say, the great profit made by barbarians is all taken from the rightful share of China. 

>By what right do they then in return use the poisonous drug to injure the Chinese people? ... Let us ask, where is your conscience? I have heard that the smoking of opium is very strictly forbidden by your country; that is because the harm caused by opium is clearly understood. Since it is not permitted to do harm to your own country, then even less should you let it be passed on to the harm of other countries - how much less to China!

Source: Lin Zexu’s Letter To Britain's Queen Victoria To Protest The Opium Trade, 1839. Public Domain. 

**Which of the following contributed most directly to the developments described in the excerpt above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: China maintained strict control over trade agreements while consumer demand for Chinese goods in Britain rose.
China’s economic dominance was waning, and the British took advantage of that weakness to gain more political control.
The military dominance of Great Britain forced China to allow the flow of opium into its ports.
The British initially sent opium to China in an effort to foster goodwill with the Chinese government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.** 

>All those people in China who sell opium or smoke opium should receive the death penalty. If we trace the crime of those barbarians who through the years have been selling opium, then the deep harm they have wrought and the great profit they have usurped should fundamentally justify their execution according to law…

>We find that your country is sixty or seventy thousand li [three li equal one mile] from China. Yet there are barbarian ships that strive to come here for trade for the purpose of making a great profit. The wealth of China is used to profit the barbarians. That is to say, the great profit made by barbarians is all taken from the rightful share of China. 

>By what right do they then in return use the poisonous drug to injure the Chinese people? ... Let us ask, where is your conscience? I have heard that the smoking of opium is very strictly forbidden by your country; that is because the harm caused by opium is clearly understood. Since it is not permitted to do harm to your own country, then even less should you let it be passed on to the harm of other countries - how much less to China!

Source: Lin Zexu’s Letter To Britain's Queen Victoria To Protest The Opium Trade, 1839. Public Domain. 

**Developments such as the one depicted in the excerpt above most directly contributed to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Century of Humiliation
the end of the opium trade in China
the beginning of the Chinese Revolution
the decade of industry

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a commodity that gave European countries and companies an economic advantage?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: copper extracted from Chile
cotton farmed in the Caribbean
opium exported from China
palm oil from Latin America
